flashcards.back: Comments are non-executable statements in Java that are completely ignored by the compiler. They serve as internal documentation to help programmers understand, maintain, and debug programs.

flashcards.front: How do you write a single-line comment in Java?
flashcards.back: Single-line comments are written using // (double forward slash). Everything after // on that line is treated as a comment.

flashcards.front: How do you write a multi-line comment in Java?
flashcards.back: Multi-line comments start with /* and end with */. Everything between these delimiters is treated as a comment and can span multiple lines.

flashcards.front: What is the syntax for documentation comments in Java?
flashcards.back: Documentation comments start with /** (slash double asterisk) and end with */. These are used for generating API documentation using the Javadoc tool.
Javadoc is a tool included with JDK that generates HTML documentation from documentation comments in Java source code. It extracts special tags like @author, @param, @return, etc.

flashcards.front: What happens to comments during compilation?
flashcards.back: Comments are completely ignored by the Java compiler. They do not affect program execution, memory usage, or performance in any way.

flashcards.front: What are the three types of comments in Java?
flashcards.back:
1. Single-line comments (//)
2. Multi-line comments (/* */)
3. Documentation comments (/** */)

flashcards.front: Which Javadoc tag is used to document method parameters?
flashcards.back: @param tag is used to document method parameters. Syntax: @param parameterName description

flashcards.front: Which Javadoc tag is used to document the return value of a method?
flashcards.back: @return tag is used to document what a method returns. Syntax: @return descriptionOfReturnValue

flashcards.front: What is the difference between /* and /** comments?
flashcards.back: /* */ are multi-line comments for internal use. /** */ are documentation comments processed by Javadoc to generate API documentation.

flashcards.front: Can comments be nested in Java?
flashcards.block: No, Java does not support nested multi-line comments. /* /* nested */ */ will cause a compilation error.
flashcards.back: Can comments be nested in Java?

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the @author Javadoc tag?
flashcards.back: @author tag specifies the author of the class or interface. It is used at the class-level and is one of the standard Javadoc tags.

flashcards.front: What is the @version Javadoc tag used for?
flashcards.back: @version tag specifies the version of the class or interface. It is typically used with @author at the class level.

flashcards.front: What is the @throws/@exception Javadoc tag used for?
flashcards.back: @throws or @exception tag documents exceptions that a method can throw. Syntax: @throws exceptionClass description

flashcards.front: Best practice: Where should documentation comments be placed?
flashcards.back: Documentation comments should be placed immediately before class, interface, method, and field declarations to generate proper API documentation.
